narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 63% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical 5.7 average; a Year 3 decile of just 2, well under the usual 5.5; lower-middle family income compared to the dataset's average bracket; an LSAT score of 24 versus the standard 37; and an undergraduate GPA of 2.4 against the norm of 3.2. You're also male, Asian, and planning full-time study—traits that align closely with the majority of students in our data. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 academic decile, which had the biggest pull toward predicting failure since it's in the lower ranks compared to peers. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, similarly pushing the prediction toward struggle due to its below-average standing. Your family income bracket came third, contributing to the risk assessment as it falls short of typical levels seen in successful bar takers. Your LSAT score ranked fourth in impact, with its low value nudging the model firmly toward expecting a fail. Your racial background as Asian followed, adding a slight but notable influence in this direction, while your undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller effects, all leaning toward higher failure odds. Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than most applicants who get approved. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 37, strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and build a stronger academic record in any advanced studies to lift those deciles. While family income and demographics are harder to shift, seeking scholarships or financial aid could help indirectly by reducing stress. With targeted improvements here, you could flip the model's outlook and strengthen your next application—many students turn things around this way. Keep pushing; your determination matters most.
